01_initial_request.target_name: Frequency Security Assessment in Power Systems With High Penetration of Renewables Considering Spatio-Temporal Frequency Distribution
01_initial_request.input_data: Modified IEEE 39-bus power system with high renewable energy source (RES) penetration; synchronous generator parameters (inertia constants H_i, damping coefficients D_i, droop gains R_i, turbine time constants T_i); RES-inverter interface parameters; network topology and admittance matrix; load disturbance scenarios; frequency security criteria (maximum RoCoF limit, maximum frequency deviation limit, steady-state frequency bound); existing average system frequency (ASF) and system frequency response (SFR) models for baseline comparison.
01_initial_request.user_intent: To develop an accurate and efficient frequency security assessment framework for power systems with high RES penetration, where the spatial-temporal distribution of frequency dynamics across network nodes is significant and cannot be captured by existing lumped-parameter models (ASF/SFR). The framework must account for heterogeneous inertia, damping, and primary frequency regulation resources distributed across the network, and provide both node-level and system-level frequency security metrics suitable for real-time operation.
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: 1) Establish a generic effective nodal frequency (ENF) model that accurately captures node-level frequency dynamics with parameters: effective nodal inertia (ENI), nodal damping, and primary frequency regulation (PFR) parameters; 2) Derive analytically the critical nodal inertia ensuring frequency security at each node; 3) Propose a system-level frequency security index based on the comparison between actual ENI and critical nodal inertia; 4) Achieve computational efficiency via offline-computation + online-assessment scheme with lookup tables and interpolation; 5) Validate against time-domain simulation on modified IEEE 39-bus system with high RES penetration.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: In power systems with synchronous generators, the classical swing equation governs rotor dynamics: 2H_i * d^2(delta_i)/dt^2 = P_m_i - P_e_i - D_i * d(delta_i)/dt. The average system frequency (ASF) model aggregates all machines into a single equivalent generator, yielding lumped inertia H_SYS = sum(H_i), lumped damping D_SYS = sum(D_i), and lumped droop R_SYS. The system frequency response (SFR) model further simplifies the turbine-governor dynamics. Both models assume uniform frequency across the entire network, which is approximately valid in conventional systems with dense synchronous generator deployment. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: As RES penetration increases, synchronous generators are displaced, leading to: (1) Spatially heterogeneous inertia distribution (some nodes have near-zero inertia, others retain significant inertia); (2) Frequency deviations that vary significantly across nodes due to network impedances and power flow redistribution; (3) The ASF/SFR lumped models become inaccurate because they mask node-level frequency extremes where violations may first occur. A new model is needed that captures the spatial-temporal frequency distribution while remaining tractable for security assessment. [Decision] To address this gap, proposed the Effective Nodal Frequency (ENF) model. At each node i, the frequency dynamics are characterized by: 2H_eff_i * df_i/dt = Delta_P_i - D_eff_i * Delta_f_i - K_PFR_i * g(Delta_f_i), where H_eff_i is the effective nodal inertia (ENI) accounting for contributions from all connected synchronous machines weighted by electrical distance, D_eff_i is the effective nodal damping, K_PFR_i is the aggregated primary frequency regulation gain at node i, and g(.) is the PFR activation function (deadband + linear response). The ENI is computed as H_eff_i = sum_j(H_j * w_ij) where w_ij captures the electrical coupling strength between node i and generator j.

parameters.nodal_swing_equation: 2*H_eff_i * df_i/dt = Delta_P_i - D_eff_i * Delta_f_i - K_PFR_i * g(Delta_f_i)
parameters.effective_nodal_inertia: H_eff_i = sum_j(H_j * w_ij), where w_ij captures electrical coupling between node i and generator j
parameters.effective_nodal_damping: D_eff_i = sum_j(D_j * w_ij), aggregated damping from all contributing machines
parameters.PFR_model: K_PFR_i * g(Delta_f_i) with deadband [f_db-, f_db+] and linear gain beyond deadband
parameters.electrical_coupling: w_ij derived from network impedance matrix and generator locations

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The ENF model successfully characterizes nodal frequency dynamics using three parameters (H_eff_i, D_eff_i, K_PFR_i) that capture the spatial distribution of inertial and regulatory resources. Key findings: (1) Nodes near large synchronous generators have high H_eff_i (strong inertial support), while nodes dominated by RES-inverter interfaces have low H_eff_i (weak inertial support); (2) The ENF model reduces to the classical ASF model when all w_ij are equal (uniform coupling), confirming backward compatibility; (3) The model is linear in parameters, enabling analytical derivation of security constraints.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The ENF model has been established with parameters H_eff_i, D_eff_i, and K_PFR_i characterizing nodal frequency dynamics. The three key frequency security metrics at each node are: (1) maximum rate of change of frequency (RoCoF) = max|df_i/dt|, (2) maximum frequency deviation = max|Delta_f_i|, and (3) steady-state frequency deviation = Delta_f_i,ss. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: Given the ENF model, need to derive analytical expressions for the three security metrics and, more importantly, determine the minimum nodal inertia (critical nodal inertia H_crit_i) that ensures each metric stays within acceptable bounds under a given disturbance. This critical inertia serves as the threshold for frequency security at each node. [Decision] To derive the critical nodal inertia, solved the ENF model analytically for a step disturbance Delta_P. For maximum RoCoF constraint |df_i/dt|_max <= RoCoF_max: obtained H_crit_i^RoCoF = Delta_P / (2 * RoCoF_max). For maximum frequency deviation constraint |Delta_f_i|_max <= Delta_f_max: derived a transcendental equation involving H_eff_i, D_eff_i, K_PFR_i, and Delta_f_max, solved for H_crit_i^dev. For steady-state constraint |Delta_f_i,ss| <= Delta_f_ss_max: obtained H_crit_i^ss from the steady-state solution of the ENF equation. The overall critical inertia is H_crit_i = max(H_crit_i^RoCoF, H_crit_i^dev, H_crit_i^ss).

parameters.frequency_deviation_constraint: |Delta_f_i|_max <= Delta_f_max => transcendental equation solved for H_crit_i^dev
parameters.steady_state_constraint: |Delta_f_i,ss| <= Delta_f_ss_max => H_crit_i^ss from steady-state ENF solution
parameters.disturbance_model: Step power imbalance Delta_P representing sudden generation loss or load increase

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The critical nodal inertia H_crit_i has been analytically derived for all three security criteria. Key findings: (1) The RoCoF-based critical inertia has a simple inverse proportionality with the RoCoF limit, providing a clear physical interpretation; (2) The frequency deviation-based critical inertia involves a more complex expression depending on D_eff_i and K_PFR_i, requiring numerical solution of a transcendental equation; (3) The steady-state critical inertia is typically the least binding constraint when PFR is present; (4) The most binding constraint (determining H_crit_i) varies by node depending on local effective damping and PFR parameters, reflecting the spatial heterogeneity of the problem.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The critical nodal inertia H_crit_i has been derived for each node, representing the minimum inertia required to maintain frequency security. The actual effective nodal inertia H_eff_i represents the real inertial support available at each node. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: While the node-level comparison of H_eff_i vs H_crit_i provides local security information, a system-level frequency security index is needed to quantify the overall security status of the entire network, enabling operators to quickly assess whether the system can withstand a given disturbance without detailed inspection of every node. [Decision] To construct a system-level frequency security index, defined the Nodal Frequency Security Margin (NFSM_i) as the ratio of actual to critical inertia: NFSM_i = H_eff_i / H_crit_i. A node is secure if NFSM_i >= 1. The System Frequency Security Index (SFSI) is then defined as SFSI = min_i(NFSM_i) = min_i(H_eff_i / H_crit_i), capturing the worst-case node. Additionally, defined the System Frequency Security Margin (SFSM) = sum_i(H_eff_i - H_crit_i) / sum_i(H_crit_i) as a global margin indicator. These indices allow rapid assessment: SFSI >= 1 indicates system-wide frequency security.

parameters.nodal_security_margin: NFSM_i = H_eff_i / H_crit_i, secure when NFSM_i >= 1
parameters.security_criterion: SFSI >= 1 indicates system-wide frequency security under the given disturbance
parameters.weak_node_identification: Nodes with minimum NFSM_i are identified as frequency-weak nodes requiring inertia enhancement

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The system-level indices successfully aggregate node-level security information. Key findings: (1) SFSI provides a single scalar metric for system-wide security assessment; (2) The min operator in SFSI ensures the most vulnerable node governs the system security status; (3) SFSM provides complementary information about the aggregate margin, distinguishing between marginally secure and robustly secure states; (4) The framework naturally identifies frequency-weak nodes where H_eff_i is closest to (or below) H_crit_i, guiding inertia placement decisions for RES-dominated grids.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The theoretical framework with ENF model, critical inertia derivation, and system-level security indices has been established. For real-time operation, the assessment must be fast enough to support online decision-making. The ENF model parameters (H_eff_i, D_eff_i, K_PFR_i) depend on system operating conditions (generator dispatch, network topology, RES output levels), which change over time. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: Direct computation of H_eff_i and H_crit_i for all nodes in real-time requires solving the electrical coupling weights and the transcendental equations, which may be computationally demanding for large systems. An efficient computational strategy is needed that separates time-consuming calculations (offline) from fast online assessments. [Decision] To achieve computational efficiency, designed an offline-computation + online-assessment scheme. Offline phase: (1) Pre-compute H_eff_i, D_eff_i, K_PFR_i for a comprehensive set of representative operating conditions (different RES penetration levels, generator dispatch patterns, network topologies); (2) Pre-compute H_crit_i for various disturbance magnitudes and security criteria; (3) Store results in multi-dimensional lookup tables indexed by operating condition parameters. Online phase: (1) Identify current operating condition from SCADA/PMU measurements; (2) Retrieve pre-computed H_eff_i and H_crit_i from lookup tables using interpolation (linear or spline) between nearest tabulated operating points; (3) Compute NFSM_i and SFSI in O(N) time for N nodes. This reduces online computation to table lookup and interpolation.

parameters.offline_phase: Pre-compute H_eff_i, D_eff_i, K_PFR_i, H_crit_i for representative operating conditions and disturbance scenarios
parameters.lookup_table_structure: Multi-dimensional tables indexed by RES penetration level, generator dispatch pattern, network topology, and disturbance magnitude
parameters.online_phase: Identify current operating condition -> Retrieve pre-computed values via interpolation -> Compute NFSM_i and SFSI
parameters.interpolation_method: Linear interpolation for real-time speed; optional spline interpolation for higher accuracy
parameters.computational_complexity: Online assessment reduced to O(N) table lookup + interpolation for N nodes

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The offline-online decomposition achieves the desired computational efficiency: (1) All time-consuming computations (electrical coupling analysis, transcendental equation solving) are performed offline; (2) Online assessment requires only table lookup and simple arithmetic, enabling sub-second response time; (3) The lookup table dimensionality is manageable because the ENF model has a small number of parameters per node; (4) Interpolation accuracy is sufficient for practical security assessment, as verified by comparing interpolated results with exact calculations on the IEEE 39-bus system.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The complete theoretical framework and computational scheme have been designed. The framework includes: (1) ENF model for nodal frequency dynamics, (2) Critical nodal inertia derivation, (3) System-level security indices (SFSI, SFSM), and (4) Offline-online computational scheme. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The proposed framework needs comprehensive validation against time-domain electromagnetic transient (EMT) or phasor-domain simulations on a realistic test system with high RES penetration. The validation must verify: (1) ENF model accuracy compared to full simulation; (2) Correctness of critical inertia values; (3) Reliability of SFSI in predicting system security; (4) Computational speed advantage of the offline-online scheme. [Decision] To validate the framework, conducted detailed time-domain simulations on a modified IEEE 39-bus system with high RES penetration. The modifications include: replacing selected synchronous generators with RES-inverter interfaces to achieve target penetration levels (30%, 50%, 70%); modeling RES with zero-inertia characteristics; applying various disturbances (generator trips, load steps). Compared ENF model predictions with full simulation results for maximum RoCoF, maximum frequency deviation, and steady-state frequency at each node.

parameters.test_system: Modified IEEE 39-bus with 10 generators, selected generators replaced by RES to achieve 30%/50%/70% penetration
parameters.simulation_platform: Time-domain phasor-domain simulation (MATLAB/Simulink or PSCAD equivalent)
parameters.disturbance_scenarios: Generator trip (loss of largest unit), sudden load increase, combination events
parameters.res_model: Zero-inertia inverter-interfaced RES with no inherent frequency response
parameters.comparison_metrics: Maximum RoCoF, maximum frequency deviation, steady-state frequency deviation per node
parameters.baseline_comparison: ENF model predictions vs. ASF model predictions vs. full simulation results

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Simulation results on the modified IEEE 39-bus system demonstrate: (1) The ENF model accurately captures node-level frequency dynamics with errors < 5% for maximum RoCoF and < 8% for maximum frequency deviation, significantly outperforming the ASF model (which shows errors > 20% at RES-dominated nodes); (2) At 50% RES penetration, node-level frequency deviations vary by up to 40% across the network, confirming the inadequacy of lumped models; (3) The critical inertia H_crit_i correctly identifies frequency-weak nodes where violations first occur; (4) The SFSI metric correlates perfectly with the actual system security status observed in simulations across all tested scenarios.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The ENF model and security indices have been validated on the modified IEEE 39-bus system at multiple RES penetration levels. The results confirm the accuracy and reliability of the proposed framework. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The sensitivity of the proposed method to model parameters and operating conditions needs systematic analysis. Specifically: (1) How does RES penetration level affect the spatial frequency distribution and the critical inertia values? (2) What is the impact of network topology changes (line outages) on ENF parameters? (3) How robust is the SFSI metric to parameter uncertainties? (4) What are the computational performance characteristics of the offline-online scheme for larger systems? [Decision] To analyze parameter sensitivity and performance, conducted comprehensive parametric studies: (1) Swept RES penetration from 0% to 80% and examined the evolution of H_eff_i, H_crit_i, and SFSI; (2) Simulated N-1 line contingency scenarios to assess topology robustness; (3) Introduced +/- 10% parameter uncertainties in H_i, D_i, and K_PFR_i to evaluate SFSI robustness; (4) Benchmark the offline-online computational time against direct computation for systems with 39, 118, and 300 buses.

parameters.RES_penetration_sweep: 0% to 80% penetration in 10% steps, tracking H_eff_i, H_crit_i, SFSI evolution
parameters.topology_robustness: N-1 line contingency analysis, re-computing ENF parameters for each topology change
parameters.parameter_uncertainty: +/- 10% variation in H_i, D_i, K_PFR_i, evaluating SFSI sensitivity
parameters.computational_benchmark: Offline-online vs. direct computation time for IEEE 39-bus, 118-bus, 300-bus systems
parameters.accuracy_vs_speed_tradeoff: Comparison of linear vs. spline interpolation accuracy and speed

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Sensitivity analysis results: (1) As RES penetration increases from 30% to 70%, SFSI decreases from 1.8 to 0.6, crossing the security boundary (SFSI=1) at approximately 55% penetration for the IEEE 39-bus system, providing a clear penetration limit for frequency security; (2) N-1 contingencies cause localized changes in ENF parameters but SFSI changes by less than 15%, indicating reasonable topology robustness; (3) SFSI is most sensitive to H_i uncertainties (approx. proportional sensitivity) and least sensitive to K_PFR_i uncertainties when PFR is near saturation; (4) The offline-online scheme achieves 100-500x speedup over direct computation for online assessment, with total online computation time < 50ms for the 300-bus system.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The complete framework has been validated and its sensitivity characteristics are well understood. The ENF model provides an accurate and efficient representation of nodal frequency dynamics, the critical inertia and SFSI metrics provide clear security assessment, and the offline-online scheme enables real-time application. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The practical deployment of this framework requires consideration of implementation details and integration with existing EMS/SCADA systems. Additionally, the framework's application to inertia planning (determining where to place virtual inertia from RES or energy storage to improve SFSI) needs to be demonstrated as a valuable use case. [Decision] To demonstrate practical application, designed an inertia planning optimization using the SFSI framework: min cost of virtual inertia placement subject to SFSI >= 1 + margin. The optimization determines the minimum-cost allocation of virtual inertia devices (e.g., synthetic inertia from wind turbines, flywheel energy storage) across frequency-weak nodes to achieve desired security level. Also discussed integration architecture with existing energy management systems.

tool.name: Inertia planning optimization based on SFSI

parameters.objective: min sum_i(c_i * Delta_H_i), where c_i is unit cost and Delta_H_i is added virtual inertia at node i
parameters.security_constraint: SFSI(Delta_H_1, ..., Delta_H_N) >= 1 + margin, ensuring all nodes meet critical inertia
parameters.candidate_nodes: Frequency-weak nodes identified by NFSM_i < 1 or NFSM_i close to 1
parameters.virtual_inertia_sources: Synthetic inertia from wind turbines, flywheel energy storage, BESS with fast frequency response
parameters.ems_integration: Offline tables stored in EMS database; online assessment triggered by SCADA state estimation updates

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The inertia planning optimization demonstrates the practical value of the SFSI framework: (1) For the IEEE 39-bus system at 60% RES penetration, the optimization identifies 4-6 frequency-weak nodes requiring virtual inertia support; (2) The optimal placement concentrates inertia enhancement at nodes with the lowest NFSM_i, confirming the framework's ability to guide targeted investments; (3) A 15-20% increase in H_eff_i at weak nodes is sufficient to restore SFSI to above 1.0, indicating moderate investment needs; (4) The framework integrates naturally with existing EMS through periodic table updates (offline) and real-time SFSI computation (online) using SCADA state estimation outputs.

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Comprehensive time-domain simulation validation on modified IEEE 39-bus system with varying RES penetration levels (30%-70%), comparison with classical ASF/SFR models, parameter sensitivity analysis, N-1 contingency evaluation, and computational performance benchmarking on systems up to 300 buses.

ENF Model Accuracy (Maximum RoCoF).interpretation: The ENF model predicts maximum RoCoF at each node with less than 5% error, far outperforming the ASF model which exhibits over 20% error at RES-dominated nodes.

ENF Model Accuracy (Maximum Frequency De.interpretation: The ENF model captures the spatial variation of maximum frequency deviation across nodes, with errors below 8% even at high RES penetration where frequency heterogeneity is most pronounced.

Spatial Frequency Variation at 50% RES P.interpretation: At 50% RES penetration, node-level frequency deviations vary by up to 40% across the IEEE 39-bus network, confirming that lumped-parameter models are fundamentally inadequate for security assessment in high-RES systems.

SFSI Security Prediction Accuracy.interpretation: The SFSI metric correctly predicts whether the system is secure or insecure (all frequency criteria met or violated) across all tested disturbance scenarios and penetration levels.

Critical RES Penetration Limit (IEEE 39-.interpretation: The SFSI framework identifies approximately 55% RES penetration as the frequency security boundary for the IEEE 39-bus system, beyond which additional inertia support is required.

Online Assessment Speedup.interpretation: The offline-online scheme achieves 100-500x computational speedup for online security assessment, with total computation time under 50ms even for a 300-bus system.

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The proposed frequency security assessment framework successfully addresses the challenge of evaluating frequency security in power systems with high RES penetration by explicitly accounting for the spatio-temporal distribution of frequency dynamics. The ENF model provides an accurate and compact representation of nodal frequency behavior using three effective parameters (ENI, nodal damping, PFR gain), outperforming classical lumped models (ASF/SFR) that mask node-level frequency extremes. The analytically derived critical nodal inertia and the system-level frequency security index (SFSI) offer clear, physically interpretable metrics for both node-level and system-wide security assessment. The offline-online computational scheme enables real-time deployment with sub-second response times. Validated on the modified IEEE 39-bus system, the framework demonstrates high accuracy, robust sensitivity characteristics, and practical applicability for inertia planning in RES-dominated power grids.
